Cultural sensitivity: honor cultural practices while protecting from harm; interpret words, not intent. Institutional abuse: facility staff abuse, restraint use, medication misuse. Long-term care regulations: minimum staffing ratios, background checks, incident reporting. Consequences of untreated abuse: increased morbidity, mortality, functional decline, caregiver burnout (ironically, abuse-stressed caregivers need support too).

CDR ≥1 → formal driving assessment required (SOE=A). Patient self-rating of driving ability is NOT useful for identifying unsafe drivers (SOE=A, GRS). MMSE alone insufficient for driving decisions. AMA Physician's Guide to Assessing and Counseling Older Drivers: comprehensive functional battery. Trail Making Test Part B: sensitive for driving-relevant executive function. Report to מרב״ד (Medical Center for Road Safety), NOT to employer. Takanah 12b (Israeli regulation): mandatory reporting to licensing authority when physician determines patient poses IMMEDIATE DANGER on road. Vision requirements: corrected acuity <6/12 in better eye. Recurrent symptomatic hypoglycemia. Uncontrolled epilepsy. Severe Parkinson's with motor fluctuations. Sedating medications: must warn patient AND document. Physician liability if failure to report leads to accident. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Driving ability declines with age and medical conditions; 15-20% of those >70 years have functional limitations affecting driving. Assessment tools: Clock Drawing Test (CDT) screens for cognitive impairment (abnormal if >2 minutes or 4+ errors); Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) or Mini-Cog; Useful Field of View (UFOV) tests processing speed and divided attention. Conditions affecting driving: dementia (early stage: can drive, reassess 6 months; moderate: generally not safe; severe: definitely unsafe), Parkinson disease (tremor, rigidity less impairing than bradykinesia/postural instability), stroke (attention, visual field defects, motor control), cardiac arrhythmias (syncope risk), hypertension (if severe), diabetes (hypoglycemia unawareness dangerous), sleep apnea (excessive daytime somnolence), depression (concentration), medications (sedatives, anticholinergics). Vision requirements: 20/40 corrected vision, visual field >120 degrees, contrast sensitivity. Medications affecting driving: benzodiazepines (sedation, impaired reaction time), opioids (drowsiness, impaired judgment), anticholinergics (confusion, blurred vision), SSRIs (initially sedating), stimulants (may improve attention in ADHD). Functional driving assessment: occupational therapist-administered behind-wheel evaluation gold standard; identifies specific deficits and compensatory strategies. Physician role: assess capacity, counsel on risks, discuss alternatives (public transit, medical transport, community rides), document discussions. Legal considerations: duty to warn (varies by state); if unsafe, report to DMV (protects confidentiality); may need to report to patient and family. Alternative transportation: ride-sharing services, public transportation, senior centers, volunteer driver programs. Advanced age alone not basis for restriction; individualize based on functional ability.

Legal Capacity and Guardianship Law (Israel): when person cannot make decisions for themselves. Types: guardian for person (health/daily decisions), property (financial), or both. Temporary or permanent court appointment. Medical opinion required. LEAST RESTRICTIVE alternative must be tried first. Continuing POA (ייפוי כוח מתמשך): appointed while person still competent, activated when capacity lost. PREFERRED over guardianship (less restrictive, respects autonomy). Activated when: patient loses decision-making capacity (e.g., delirium during sepsis requiring emergency surgery). NOT activated when: patient is competent even if elderly/hard-of-hearing/disagrees with family. תשוש נפש (cognitively impaired for placement): patient walks around, gets lost, can't find way back → MEETS criteria. NOT: cooperative patient who agrees to institutional placement. NOT: acute delirium (reversible — treat first, then reassess). 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Guardianship is court-imposed legal arrangement where guardian makes decisions for incapacitated person (ward) who cannot make decisions. Types: full guardianship (all decisions), limited guardianship (specific decisions), conservatorship (finances only), power of attorney (voluntary, pre-incapacity). Capacity for purposes of guardianship: ability to understand health/safety decisions, appreciate consequences, express wishes, use logical reasoning. Standard of proof: clear and convincing evidence of incapacity (higher than preponderance, lower than beyond reasonable doubt). Procedures: petition to probate court, legal notice to alleged incapacitated person, right to attorney, hearing before judge, testimony about capacity. Alternatives to guardianship (preferred if possible): durable power of attorney (patient appoints agent while still capable), HIPAA authorization (allows healthcare information sharing), joint bank account, representative payee for Social Security. Rights of ward: still entitled to medical care, representation, due process, least restrictive alternative. Duties of guardian: act in ward's best interest, avoid conflicts of interest, make regular accountings to court, respect ward's preferences and values when known. Drawbacks: expensive ($2000-5000 court costs plus ongoing fees), invasive (public proceedings), irreversible, all-or-nothing (except limited guardianship). Abuse risk: some guardians exploit wards financially. Review: periodic court review required (varies by state); guardianship should terminate if capacity restored. Ethical considerations: respect autonomy even with cognitive impairment; least restrictive alternative preferred; shared decision-making when possible.

Core rights: Informed consent (explain diagnosis, options, risks, benefits in patient's language/comprehension level). Right to refuse ANY treatment — even life-saving — if competent (MUST document). Right to privacy and medical confidentiality (exceptions: legal requirement, public health). Right to access full medical records. Right to second opinion. Emergency exception: if patient incapacitated + life-threatening delay → treat without explicit consent (implied consent doctrine). Family CANNOT override a competent patient's wishes — even if family disagrees, patient's autonomy prevails. Minor's consent: generally parents consent, but minor can consent independently for certain conditions. Mental health: special protections under separate legislation. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Patient rights framework: dignity, privacy, autonomy, respect, access to care, grievance procedures. HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act): federal law protecting health information privacy. Patient Selfetermination Act (PSDA) 1990: mandates healthcare facilities offer advance directives and discuss end-of-life preferences; applies to hospitals, nursing homes, hospice, HMOs. State laws vary significantly: consent laws, right-to-refuse-treatment, right-to-die statutes. Informed consent: right to full disclosure before treatment; exceptions in emergency when consent impossible. Right to refuse treatment: competent patient can refuse any treatment, even lifesaving; applies to transfusion (important in Jehovah's Witnesses), experimental drugs, surgery. Incompetent patient: surrogate must refuse based on substituted judgment (what patient would want) or best interests (what is objectively best). Confidentiality: physician-patient privilege legal in most states; exceptions include child/elder abuse, threats of serious harm, communicable disease. ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act): requires reasonable accommodations for disabilities; applies to healthcare access. Fair Housing Act: governs residential settings; applies to assisted living, memory care. Long-term care regulations: residents have rights to competent care, dignity, respect, freedom from abuse/restraint, participation in care decisions. Institutional review: ombudsman advocates for residents in long-term care; available to investigate complaints. Sexual rights of elderly: nursing home residents have right to sexual expression and privacy. Grievance procedures: required in all healthcare settings; must be expeditious, non-retaliation. Medical records access: patient right to inspect and copy medical records; right to request amendments and addendums. Billing rights: right to understand charges, request itemization, dispute billing errors. Advance directive validity: requirements vary by state (e.g., witness requirements, notarization); out-of-state directives may not be recognized; recommend addressing same-state validity in advance directive.

Dying Patient Act (2005): applies when life expectancy ≤6 months and illness is irreversible. Patient can be declared 'dying' (נוטה למות) by two physicians. Withholding NEW treatment: permitted per patient's wishes or advance directive. Withdrawing ONGOING treatment: MORE RESTRICTED — timer mechanism for continuous treatments (e.g., ventilator set to cycle off and be re-evaluated). Advance directives (הנחיות מקדימות): must be in writing, witnessed by 2 witnesses, valid 5 years, renewable. Can appoint proxy decision-maker. Pain management: MUST provide adequate analgesia even if may hasten death (double effect doctrine — intention is symptom relief, not death). Cannot actively hasten death (euthanasia illegal in Israel). Palliative sedation: permitted for refractory symptoms at end of life. Physician-assisted suicide: not legal in Israel. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Advance Directive: legal document in which person (while competent) expresses wishes about future medical care if unable to communicate. Two main components: Living Will (specifies treatments wanted/unwanted) and Healthcare Power of Attorney/Proxy (designates surrogate). Living Will addresses: CPR, intubation, feeding tubes, mechanical ventilation, dialysis, antibiotics, comfort care only. Components of valid advance directive: (1) written document, (2) patient signature, (3) signed/dated, (4) witnessed (requirements vary by state; some require notarization), (5) clear, unambiguous language. POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) form: medical order (not just document); signed by physician and patient; portable across settings; addresses resuscitation status (full resuscitation/limited interventions/comfort measures), level of care (full treatment/limited acute/palliative). Differences from advance directive: POLST is actionable physician order, advance directive is statement of wishes (physician must write orders based on it). Healthcare proxy: designated person (must accept role) to make medical decisions if patient unable. Proxy selection considerations: knows patient's values, willing to advocate, available, not conflicted. Discussion before crisis: ideally done when patient healthy and thinking clearly; enables clarification of wishes. Updated regularly: circumstances change; review annually or if major life event. Completion: many forms available (state-specific); discuss with family and healthcare provider; copy to all clinicians; consider wallet card with key directives; some states have registry. Limitations: cannot request illegal activity or hastening death (except in jurisdictions with medical aid in dying); cannot override surrogate decision-making authority (once incapacitated). Revocation: patient can always change/revoke advance directive; can override it verbally if still competent. Non-compliance: if physician unwilling to honor advance directive, obligation to inform patient and assist transfer to willing physician. Conflicts: if living will and proxy disagree, proxy's interpretation typically prevails.

Models: home care (most preferred by patients), adult day centers (social + medical monitoring + respite), assisted living, nursing homes (skilled nursing + rehabilitation). Hospital-at-home: substitute for acute hospital admission — shown to reduce costs + improve quality (SOE=A, Hazzard). CAPABLE program (OT + nurse + handyperson): reduces disability and inpatient costs. MIND at Home: dementia care coordination with community health workers — reduces costs. Preventive home visits: identify hidden risks in community-dwelling elderly. Israeli system: Kupat Holim provides primary care, Bituach Leumi (social security) for nursing/home care benefits. Geriatrician assessment required for home services eligibility in age ≥90 (Israeli policy). COVID-19: devastating impact on nursing homes — highlighted need for infection control, staffing, telehealth, advance care planning in LTC facilities. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Long-term care (LTC) settings: independent living (no care services), assisted living (ADLs help), intermediate care (daily nursing), skilled nursing facility (SNF, Medicare-covered rehab), continuing care retirement communities (CCRC, all levels in one location). Continuum approach: match level of care to functional/cognitive status; transitions as needs change. Home care: professional (nursing, PT/OT, aide services) and informal (family); most elderly prefer aging-in-place. Program of All-Inclusive Care for Elderly (PACE): integrated managed care serving frail elderly; enrollment criteria typically age >55, Medicaid/Medicare eligible, nursing home level of care needs; all services (medical, social, transportation) provided. Assessments in LTC: Minimum Data Set (MDS) required in nursing homes; functional status (ADLs/IADLs), cognitive status, behavioral symptoms, medical conditions. Care planning: multidisciplinary team (physician, RN, social worker, family) develops individualized plan. Staffing requirements: vary by state; nursing home minimum 1:4 RN to resident ratio, varies for aides. Quality indicators: infection rates, hospital readmission, antipsychotic use, restraint use, pressure ulcers, pain management, care processes (medications, assessments). CMS surveying: annual unannounced surveys, state-specific surveyors; violations result in fines, mandatory corrections, potential loss of certification. Transitions of care: discharge planning, medication reconciliation, communication between settings critical; high readmission risk first 30 days post-discharge. Advance directives in LTC: must be addressed on admission; facility must honor resident wishes. Rehabilitation potential: not all LTC is permanent; some residents discharged home after recovery. Social aspects: visiting, activities programming, spiritual care important to quality of life. Cost: nursing home ~$100,000/year (varies regionally); many bankrupted by LTC costs; Medicaid covers only after assets depleted; long-term care insurance rare.

FIM (Functional Independence Measure): 18 items (13 motor + 5 cognitive), score 18-126. Motor FIM gain = primary outcome measure. Required for Israeli rehabilitation admission criteria (אמות מידה). Rehab eligibility: must have rehabilitation potential, medical stability, ability to participate (tolerate ~3h/day therapy), identified goals. Hip fracture: surgery within 48h, early mobilization day 1 postop with weight-bearing as tolerated (do NOT restrict to partial weight-bearing — evidence does not support delayed mobilization, SOE=B). Orthogeriatric co-management: fewer complications, shorter stays, lower costs, improved function (SOE=A). Stroke: early rehab (within 24-48h when stable) improves outcomes. Cardiac rehab: beneficial even in elderly with HF. Deconditioning: early mobilization in hospital prevents — hospital-acquired disability is common and preventable. Walking aids: cane on OPPOSITE side of pathology. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Rehabilitation goal: maximize functional independence and quality of life after illness/injury. Indications: stroke, hip fracture, cardiac event, pulmonary disease, joint replacement, amputation, spinal cord injury, general deconditioning. Setting: inpatient (hospital, rehabilitation facility), outpatient (clinic, home-based). Functional assessment: ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, feeding, continence, mobility), IADLs (cooking, laundry, finances, medication management), ambulation, cognition. FIM (Functional Independence Measure) scale: rates 18 domains from 1 (total assistance) to 7 (complete independence). Post-acute care timing: acute rehabilitation facility (ARF) for intensive therapy (3+ hours daily), skilled nursing facility (SNF) for moderate therapy, home health/outpatient for lower-intensity. Physical therapy: strengthening, balance, gait training, assistive device training (walker, cane, wheelchair); addresses fall prevention. Occupational therapy: ADL/IADL training, adaptive equipment, home modifications (grab bars, ramps, accessible bathroom). Speech/language pathology: swallowing assessment (dysphagia), speech therapy if aphasia/dysarthria. Psychological support: depression common post-stroke/MI/amputation (20-30% develop major depression); early intervention improves outcomes. Cognitive rehabilitation: memory strategies, problem-solving, executive function training; benefit limited in dementia. Cardiac rehabilitation: supervised exercise, education, risk factor modification post-MI/revascularization; improves outcomes and reduces mortality by 20%. Pulmonary rehabilitation: exercise training, breathing techniques, disease education; improves dyspnea and exercise tolerance even in COPD. Neuroplasticity: brain's ability to rewire after stroke; optimal window 3-6 months but improvement possible years out with intensive therapy. Prognosis: depends on severity, age, cognition, comorbidities, motivation; elderly achieve functional gains similar to younger if adequate rehabilitation provided. Discharge planning: assess safety, arrange equipment/home services, caregiver training, follow-up. Return to work: many elderly not employed; focus on ADLs/community reintegration instead.

Presbycusis (age-related hearing loss): HIGH-frequency loss first → difficulty distinguishing consonants. Words with high-frequency sounds (like 'גלידה' with hard 'ג') harder to hear than low-frequency words ('שלום'). Screening: whispered voice test at 2 feet, audiometry. Hearing aids: underutilized despite proven benefit for communication, cognition, social engagement. Lancet 2020 Commission: HEARING LOSS = #1 modifiable midlife risk factor for dementia. AMD (age-related macular degeneration): leading cause of irreversible vision loss >65y. Dry (atrophic): 90% of cases, gradual central vision loss, drusen. Wet (neovascular): anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept). Cataracts: most common cause of reversible visual impairment; surgery highly effective, can be done under local anesthesia. Glaucoma: screen IOP + optic disc; open-angle most common; treat with prostaglandin analogs (latanoprost). Low vision rehabilitation: magnifiers, contrast enhancement, lighting optimization. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Vision changes with aging: presbyopia (decline in accommodation) by age 50 makes reading difficult without glasses; reduced pupil size reduces retinal illumination by 2-3x at age 60 vs 20; increased lens yellowing reduces blue color perception. Cataracts: cloudiness of lens; affects 50% by age 65, 75% by age 85; causes progressive blurring, glare, reduced night vision; treatment is surgery when visually significant; artificial intraocular lens now standard. Glaucoma: elevated intraocular pressure (>21 mmHg) damages optic nerve; affects 3-4% of those >60 years; often asymptomatic until significant vision loss (silent thief of sight); screening with tonometry, optic nerve appearance; treatment with prostaglandin analogs (latanoprost), beta-blockers (timolol), carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide), alpha-agonists, oral agents if drops fail; goal is IOP reduction by 20-30%. Age-related macular degeneration (AMD): leading cause of blindness in elderly >65; affects central vision. Dry AMD (80%): drusen deposits; loss of photoreceptors; no treatment proven effective, though zinc/antioxidants sometimes recommended. Wet AMD (20%): choroidal neovascularization; worse prognosis; anti-VEGF injections (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept) mainstay; monthly injections. Diabetic retinopathy: affects 1/3 of diabetics; screening with dilated eye exam annually; treatment with laser, anti-VEGF, vitrectomy if macular edema/proliferation. Visual impairment impact: falls (3x increased risk), depression, cognitive decline, driving cessation, reduced independence. Hearing loss affects 65% of those >70 years; conductive hearing loss (cerumen impaction, otosclerosis) often correctable; sensorineural hearing loss (noise exposure, aging, genetics) usually irreversible. Age-related hearing loss (presbycusis): bilateral symmetrical high-frequency loss; often associated with tinnitus; leads to social isolation if untreated. Screening: simple question 'Do you feel you have a hearing problem?'; Whisper Test or Weber/Rinne examination; formal audiometry if impairment suspected. Cerumen impaction: extremely common; simple removal (curette, irrigation) improves hearing dramatically. Hearing aids: appropriate if pure-tone average >30 dB; newer devices have directional microphones, telecoils, Bluetooth; acceptance rates ~20% due to stigma, cost, difficulty with insertion/battery changes in arthritic hands. Cochlear implant: for severe-to-profound bilateral hearing loss not helped by aids; surgery with good outcomes in selected elderly. Communication strategies: face patient, speak clearly (don't shout), reduce background noise, written communication if needed. Hearing loss and cognitive impairment: hearing loss associated with accelerated cognitive decline; hearing aid use may slow progression.

CGA pre-op improves cancer surgery outcomes. Hip fracture: surgery within 48h — delay in otherwise stable patients worsens outcomes (SOE=B). Unnecessary preoperative cardiac testing may itself be an independent risk factor for complications in hip fracture (delays surgery). ACC/AHA 2014 periop guidelines: continue beta-blockers if already taking (SOE=B), do NOT start on day of surgery. Pre-op: Revised Cardiac Risk Index (Lee index). Dementia DOUBLES post-op delirium risk (56% vs 26%, GRS). Diabetes doubles perioperative complications. Delirium prevention: HELP protocol, avoid anticholinergics, early catheter removal, family presence, sleep hygiene. DVT prophylaxis: mandatory (LMWH or fondaparinux). Medication management: hold metformin 48h pre-op, sulfonylureas morning of, basal insulin at 50% dose, hold ACEi/ARB morning of surgery (hypotension risk under anesthesia). DES stent: maintain dual antiplatelet per cardiology guidance (minimum 6-12mo). Hypothermia prevention: forced-air warming. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Preoperative evaluation: comprehensive history/physical focusing on cardiac (CAD, arrhythmia, HF), pulmonary (COPD, OSA, aspiration risk), metabolic (diabetes, renal disease). Cardiac risk: estimate with revised cardiac risk index (RCRI); major predictors include CAD, HF, cerebrovascular disease, diabetes, renal disease; surgery type matters (vascular surgery highest risk). Testing: EKG if age >65 or cardiac risk factors; further testing (stress test, echo) only if abnormal findings or high-risk surgery planned. Medications pre-op: continue beta-blockers, ACE-I/ARB, statins perioperatively; hold anticoagulants per guidelines (warfarin 3-5 days pre-op, DOACs 24-48 h pre-op, aspirin usually continued); metformin hold day of surgery if significant renal impairment or volume contrast planned; NSAIDs avoided. Anesthesia: type chosen by anesthesiologist; regional (spinal, epidural) reduces delirium risk compared to general anesthesia in some studies; spinal anesthesia contraindicated if thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy, or bacteremia. Antibiotic prophylaxis: cefazolin (gram-positive coverage) 1-2g IV at induction; redose if >2 hours surgery; d/c within 24h post-op (48h for cardiac surgery). DVT prophylaxis: mechanical (sequential compression devices, early mobilization) and/or pharmacologic (enoxaparin SC, warfarin, fondaparinux, rivaroxaban) depending on surgery type and bleeding risk. Glucose control: target 140-180 mg/dL; tight control (<110) increases hypoglycemia risk; insulin infusion often used in hospital. Delirium prevention: modifiable risk factors include avoiding benzodiazepines (use anticholinergic instead), optimizing oxygenation, maintaining sleep-wake cycle, early mobilization, minimizing medications, addressing pain. Delirium incidence: 10-15% post-op in general surgery, 50% post-op in major vascular surgery; worse prognosis if prolonged. Pain management: multimodal analgesia (regional block, opioid, non-opioid); caution with opioids due to respiratory depression, constipation, delirium risk. Complications: pneumonia (incentive spirometry, early mobilization reduce risk), ileus (oral intake as tolerated), infection, bleeding, thromboembolic disease. Discharge planning: assess functional status, arrange rehabilitation if needed, ensure medication reconciliation, VTE prophylaxis at discharge if appropriate.

Atypical presentations: MI without chest pain (up to 40% in >75y), infection without fever, appendicitis without classic RLQ pain, PE without tachycardia. Delirium as presenting complaint for UTI, pneumonia, MI, medication toxicity, constipation. Polypharmacy: common cause of ED visits (adverse drug events). Falls: #1 injury-related ED visit in elderly. Screening tools: CAM/4AT for delirium, ISAR (Identification of Seniors at Risk) for adverse outcomes. EQUiPPED protocol: pharmacist + physician education reduces Beers-listed prescribing in ED. Beers criteria: may have different risk-benefit in short-term ED prescriptions vs chronic use. Disposition: CGA-based ED assessment reduces readmissions. Observation units useful for elderly with syncope, HF exacerbation, chest pain rule-out. Typhlitis: neutropenic cecal inflammation in chemo patients (5-FU classic) — presents like appendicitis but requires medical (not surgical) management. Senior Aid Tool: ED screening for elder abuse. 

📖 HAZZARD'S 8e BOARD PEARLS: Geriatric emergency presentations: atypical; serious illness may present subtly without classic symptoms. Delirium: most common acute alteration in mental status in ED; assess with CAM-ICU or 4AT score; causes include infection (UTI, pneumonia), medication (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids), metabolic (hyponatremia, hypoxia, hypoglycemia), stroke, MI. Falls: 25% of community-dwelling elderly fall annually; 50% of nursing home residents fall; risk factors include weakness, balance disorder, cognitive impairment, medications (psychotropics, antihypertensives), home hazards. Syncope: 48% of elderly with syncope are due to orthostatic hypotension, 20% cardiac (arrhythmia, aortic stenosis, MI), 10% seizure, remainder unclear; workup includes orthostatic vital signs, EKG, troponin if concerning features. Acute coronary syndrome: atypical presentations common (dyspnea, confusion, syncope without chest pain); troponin more sensitive than EKG; aggressive antiplatelet therapy/anticoagulation appropriate even in very elderly if fit. Sepsis: diagnostic challenge; criteria (qSOFA score) less specific in elderly; inflammatory markers elevated even with minor infection; aggressive early antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, vasopressors if needed. Stroke/TIA: thrombolytic therapy window 4.5 h for IV-tPA; mechanical thrombectomy up to 24h if core mismatch; time-dependent; CT scan to rule out hemorrhage. Acute kidney injury: causes include dehydration, nephrotoxic medications (NSAIDs, ACE-I), contrast exposure, sepsis; prerenal AKI improves with hydration; intrinsic AKI requires renal-protective measures; postrenal AKI requires decompression. Medication errors: common in ED; elderly on multiple drugs at baseline; polypharmacy complicates treatment; avoid anticholinergics, benzodiazepines if possible. Disposition: admission vs discharge depends on functional status, living situation, support system; early palliative medicine consultation appropriate if trajectory decline. Avoiding unnecessary admission: brief observation unit appropriate for some presentations; clear safety plan essential if discharged.
